articles.title: Building Genie: Lessons from Shipping an Autonomous AI Engineer
articles.excerpt: What happens when you try to build an AI that writes production code without human supervision? We shipped Genie at Cosine AI and learned hard lessons about context windows, agent reliability, and the tyranny of synthetic data.
articles.content:
The Promise and the Problem

In late 2024, we set out to build Genie: an autonomous AI software engineer that could read a codebase, understand requirements, and ship production code. Not a copilot. Not an assistant. An agent that actually commits and pushes.

The theory was simple: give an LLM access to a repository, a set of tools (read file, write file, run tests, grep), and enough reasoning capability to plan and execute. The reality was a gauntlet of edge cases, hallucinations, and the fundamental limitations of current LLM architecture.

Context Windows Are the Bottleneck

Here's the brutal truth: modern LLMs have context windows measured in hundreds of thousands of tokens. That sounds like a lot until you're dealing with a production codebase.

A 500K token context window sounds massive. Then you try to load a moderate-sized repo and realize you've blown past 2M tokens before you've even started reasoning.

We spent months on context compression strategies. Summary trees, relevance scoring, repository indexing. The breakthrough came when we stopped trying to feed the entire codebase into every turn and instead built an agent that could ask for what it needed.

Synthetic Data: Necessary Evil

To train Genie to reason about code changes, we needed training data. Lots of it. Real commit histories are messy, noisy, full of "fix typo" and "wip". Synthetic data promised clean, curated examples of proper software engineering.

But synthetic data has a death spiral:

  • Your model generates training data
  • You train on that data
  • Your model gets slightly worse at the edges
  • You generate more data to compensate
  • Repeat until your model is a pale imitation of itself

We had to implement strict verification pipelines. Every synthetic example had to compile, pass tests, and be validated by a separate judge model. The cost was enormous. The alternative was a model that confidently generated broken code.

The Reliability Tax

Language models are probabilistic. Software engineering is deterministic. This mismatch is the core tension in autonomous coding agents.

When Genie had a 95% success rate on simple tasks, we celebrated. Then we realized that meant 1 in 20 commits would need human intervention. At scale, that's unsustainable. We built rollback mechanisms, sandboxed execution environments, and multiple verification layers. Each added latency. Each reduced the "autonomous" part of the equation.

What Actually Worked

After a year of iteration, the architecture that emerged:

  1. Hierarchical Planning: Break tasks into milestones, milestones into steps, steps into tool calls
  2. Speculative Execution: Run tests before committing, catch errors early
  3. Human-in-the-Loop Gates: High-stakes operations (deletions, refactors) require approval
  4. Continuous Learning: Every failure becomes training data for the next iteration

The Road Ahead

Genie shipped. It writes code, it commits, it sometimes even gets it right. But the gap between "sometimes right" and "reliable engineer" is vast. We're not there yet. The next frontier isn't bigger models—it's better tooling, better context management, and better understanding of when to ask for help.

The autonomous future is coming. It's just going to take longer than the hype suggested.

articles.title: jalla: Universal LLM Middleware in Go
articles.excerpt: Switching between OpenAI, Anthropic, and Gemini shouldn't require rewriting your codebase. jalla is a single binary that provides an OpenAI-compatible API for every major LLM provider.
articles.content:
The Provider Fragmentation Problem

You start with OpenAI. GPT-4 works great. Then Claude drops and suddenly it's better at reasoning. Then Gemini 1.5 Pro arrives with a 2M token context window. Your code is now a mess of different client libraries, different request formats, different response shapes.

Every provider implements their own API. They're all roughly the same, but different enough to break your code.

We needed a universal adapter. One endpoint. One request format. Switch providers by changing a config flag.

Architecture: The Adapter Pattern at Scale

jalla is built around a simple interface:

type Provider interface {
    Complete(ctx context.Context, req CompletionRequest) (*CompletionResponse, error)
    StreamComplete(ctx context.Context, req CompletionRequest) (chan StreamChunk, error)
    Embed(ctx context.Context, req EmbeddingRequest) (*EmbeddingResponse, error)
}

Each provider implements this interface, translating between OpenAI's request/response format and their native APIs. The HTTP layer never knows which provider it's talking to.

Go Implementation

The core translation layer for Anthropic looks like this:

func (a *AnthropicAdapter) Complete(ctx context.Context, req CompletionRequest) (*CompletionResponse, error) {
    // Convert OpenAI messages to Anthropic format
    anthropicReq := anthropic.MessagesRequest{
        Model:     mapModel(req.Model),
        MaxTokens: req.MaxTokens,
        Messages:  convertMessages(req.Messages),
        System:    extractSystem(req.Messages),
    }
    
    resp, err := a.client.CreateMessages(ctx, anthropicReq)
    if err != nil {
        return nil, fmt.Errorf("anthropic api error: %w", err)
    }
    
    // Convert back to OpenAI format
    return &CompletionResponse{
        ID:      resp.ID,
        Object:  "chat.completion",
        Created: time.Now().Unix(),
        Model:   req.Model,
        Choices: []Choice{{
            Index: 0,
            Message: Message{
                Role:    "assistant",
                Content: resp.Content[0].Text,
            },
            FinishReason: string(resp.StopReason),
        }},
    }, nil
}

Why Go?

Go's concurrency model is perfect for this. Thousands of concurrent streams, each handled by a lightweight goroutine. No callback hell. No event loops. Just clean, blocking code that the runtime schedules efficiently.

The binary is a single static executable. No runtime dependencies. No Python environment hell. Deploy it anywhere.

Usage

# Point at jalla instead of OpenAI
export OPENAI_BASE_URL=http://localhost:8080/v1
export OPENAI_API_KEY=jalla-api-key

# Use any OpenAI-compatible client
curl http://localhost:8080/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $OPENAI_API_KEY" \
  -d '{
    "model": "claude-3-5-sonnet",
    "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Hello"}]
  }'

Production Hardening

Real deployments need more than just translation:

  • Rate Limiting: Token bucket per API key, per model, per IP
  • Retry Logic: Exponential backoff with jitter for provider failures
  • Circuit Breakers: Fail fast when a provider is down
  • Streaming: SSE streams that multiplex multiple provider connections

jalla handles all of this transparently. Your application code stays clean.

The Result

Switching from GPT-4 to Claude to Gemini is now a configuration change. A/B test models in production. Route high-context tasks to Gemini, reasoning tasks to Claude, cost-sensitive tasks to local models. One codebase, infinite flexibility.

Sometimes the most valuable code is the code that disappears.

articles.title: beamline: Event Streaming at Scale
articles.excerpt: Kafka wasn't enough. When you're pushing 10M events per second with sub-10ms latency requirements, you need something purpose-built. This is how we built beamline in Go.
articles.content:
Why Kafka Failed Us

Kafka is a masterpiece of engineering. It handles millions of messages, provides strong durability guarantees, and has an ecosystem to match. But it's designed for log storage, not real-time event distribution.

Our requirements were different:

  • 10M+ events per second sustained throughput
  • Sub-10ms end-to-end latency (p99)
  • Dynamic fan-out: one event to thousands of consumers
  • No persistence: events are ephemeral, loss is acceptable

We tried to bend Kafka to our will. We tweaked batch sizes, partition counts, consumer configurations. At 5M events/sec, latency spiked to 200ms. The JVM garbage collector was fighting us. We needed a different approach.

Design Principles

beamline is built on three core ideas:

  1. Zero-copy networking: Events touch memory once, then hit the wire
  2. Lock-free data structures: No mutex contention on the hot path
  3. NUMA-aware allocation: Pin goroutines to cores, eliminate cache thrashing

The Architecture

A beamline cluster consists of brokers and routers. Brokers handle ingestion. Routers handle distribution. The separation allows independent scaling.

// Producer publishes to any broker
producer.Publish(event, routingKey)

// Broker stores in memory-mapped ring buffer
broker.ingest(event)

// Router subscribes to brokers, fans out to consumers
router.subscribe(pattern, consumerChan)

// Consumer receives filtered events
for event := range consumerChan {
    process(event)
}

Ring Buffers and Cache Lines

The heart of beamline is a lock-free ring buffer implementation optimized for x86 cache lines (64 bytes):

type RingBuffer struct {
    buffer   []Event
    writeIdx atomic.Uint64
    readIdx  atomic.Uint64
    mask     uint64
}

func (r *RingBuffer) Write(e Event) bool {
    idx := r.writeIdx.Add(1) - 1
    pos := idx & r.mask
    
    // Spin until slot is free (lock-free)
    for !r.buffer[pos].IsEmpty() {
        runtime.Gosched()
    }
    
    r.buffer[pos] = e
    return true
}

Each event is exactly 64 bytes, aligning perfectly with cache lines. False sharing eliminated. Contention reduced to atomic increments.

Networking: epoll on Steroids

Standard Go net/http couldn't keep up. We built on gnet—an event-driven networking framework using epoll on Linux and kqueue on BSD.

A single beamline broker handles 2M events/sec per core. With 16 cores, that's 32M events/sec per machine. We run 3 brokers for redundancy.

Latency Distribution

In production:

  • p50 latency: 2.3ms
  • p99 latency: 8.7ms
  • p99.9 latency: 15ms

The p99.9 outliers are usually GC pauses in consumer applications, not beamline itself.

When to Use beamline

beamline is not a Kafka replacement. It makes tradeoffs:

FeatureKafkabeamline
DurabilityStrong (disk)None (memory)
Latency100ms+<10ms
ThroughputHighVery High
Consumer groupsYesNo
ReplayYesNo

Use Kafka when you need durability. Use beamline when you need speed and can tolerate loss.

Lessons Learned

Building high-performance systems in Go requires understanding the runtime. The garbage collector is your friend until it isn't. Avoid allocations on hot paths. Pool your objects. Profile relentlessly.

Sometimes the right tool doesn't exist. Build it.

articles.title: gut: Building Gomputer Use Tooling
articles.excerpt: Computer use for AI agents is the new frontier. gut is a Go-based framework for building AI agents that can see screens, click buttons, and type like humans.
articles.content:
The Computer Use Revolution

LLMs can write code, but can they use software? The computer use paradigm—giving AI agents the ability to see screens and interact with UIs—opens entirely new capabilities. Customer support that actually clicks through your app. Automated testing that navigates like a real user. Data entry bots that work with legacy systems.

Computer use isn't about replacing human judgment. It's about extending AI from the text domain into the visual, interactive world.

Most computer use implementations are Python-based, resource-heavy, and hard to deploy. We built gut in Go for production environments.

Core Abstractions

gut provides three primitives:

// Computer represents a virtual desktop
type Computer interface {
    Screenshot(ctx context.Context) (*Screenshot, error)
    Click(x, y int) error
    Type(text string) error
    Key(key string) error
    Scroll(direction Direction, amount int) error
}

// Agent reasons about actions
type Agent interface {
    Observe(ctx context.Context, screenshot *Screenshot) (*Action, error)
    Learn(experience Experience) error
}

// Task is a high-level goal
type Task interface {
    Done(screenshot *Screenshot) (bool, error)
    Reward() float64
}

Vision: Beyond OCR

Early computer use systems relied on OCR—extracting text from screenshots and reasoning about positions. This fails with icons, diagrams, and modern web apps where text is often SVG.

gut uses a vision-language model (VLM) that processes screenshots directly. The model outputs structured JSON describing UI elements and their locations:

{
  "elements": [
    {
      "type": "button",
      "text": "Submit",
      "bbox": [120, 340, 200, 380],
      "confidence": 0.97
    },
    {
      "type": "input",
      "placeholder": "Email address",
      "bbox": [120, 280, 400, 320],
      "confidence": 0.94
    }
  ]
}

Execution Loop

The agent runs a simple loop:

  1. Capture screenshot
  2. VLM processes image → UI elements
  3. LLM reasons about goal → action
  4. Execute action on computer
  5. Check if task is complete
  6. Repeat
func (a *Agent) Run(ctx context.Context, task Task) error {
    for {
        screenshot, _ := a.computer.Screenshot(ctx)
        
        if done, _ := task.Done(screenshot); done {
            return nil
        }
        
        action, _ := a.Observe(ctx, screenshot)
        if err := action.Execute(a.computer); err != nil {
            a.Learn(Experience{Action: action, Error: err})
        }
        
        time.Sleep(a.pollInterval)
    }
}

Resilience Strategies

Computer use is brittle. A button moves by 5 pixels and your script breaks. gut implements several resilience patterns:

  • Visual anchors: Find reference points that rarely change
  • Retry with backoff: Elements might not be ready immediately
  • Action verification: Screenshot after action to confirm effect
  • Failure recovery: If stuck, try alternative approaches

Deployment Models

gut supports multiple deployment targets:

  • Docker: Headless Chromium in container
  • VM: Full desktop environment
  • Browser Extension: Agent runs in user's actual browser
  • SaaS: Managed browser farms

Security Considerations

Running AI agents that can click and type is a security nightmare. gut implements strict sandboxing:

  • Network isolation (no external calls)
  • Filesystem restrictions (read-only or tmpfs)
  • Rate limiting on sensitive operations
  • Audit logging of all actions

The Future

Computer use is in its infancy. Today's agents can navigate simple UIs. Tomorrow they'll handle complex multi-step workflows, adapt to layout changes, and collaborate with human operators. gut is our bet on that future—fast, reliable, and deployable at scale.

articles.title: Stream YouTube Videos Synchronously Remotely
articles.excerpt: Using Twilio Sync and WebRTC to build syncvid.io: a Chrome extension that lets friends watch YouTube together, perfectly in sync, from anywhere in the world.
articles.content:
The Problem: Long Distance Movie Nights

My partner and I were in a long-distance relationship. We'd try to watch movies "together"—pressing play at the same time over a phone call. It never worked. One person would laugh at a joke 3 seconds before the other. The experience was broken.

There had to be a better way than "3... 2... 1... play!"

The Solution: Twilio Sync

Twilio had just launched Sync—a real-time state synchronization service. Instead of building WebSocket infrastructure, I could use Sync to keep video state in perfect alignment across browsers.

The architecture was elegant:

  1. Each user installs a Chrome extension on YouTube
  2. Extension joins a Sync document (essentially a shared JSON object)
  3. When User A pauses, the pause event is written to Sync
  4. Sync broadcasts to all connected clients
  5. User B's video pauses at the exact same timestamp

Chrome Extension Architecture

The extension uses content scripts to inject into YouTube pages:

// content.js - injected into YouTube pages
const video = document.querySelector('video');

// Listen for local video events
video.addEventListener('play', () => {
  syncDocument.update({
    action: 'play',
    timestamp: video.currentTime,
    time: Date.now()
  });
});

video.addEventListener('pause', () => {
  syncDocument.update({
    action: 'pause',
    timestamp: video.currentTime
  });
});

// Listen for remote events
syncDocument.on('updated', (data) => {
  if (data.action === 'play') {
    const latency = Date.now() - data.time;
    video.currentTime = data.timestamp + (latency / 1000);
    video.play();
  } else if (data.action === 'pause') {
    video.pause();
    video.currentTime = data.timestamp;
  }
});

Handling Latency

The tricky part: network latency. When User A presses play, it takes time for that message to reach User B. By the time User B receives it, User A's video has advanced.

The solution: timestamp every action and adjust on receipt. If User A sends a play at T=0 with timestamp 30.5s, and User B receives it 100ms later, User B jumps to 30.6s before playing.

The Result

syncvid.io was born. Friends could create rooms, share links, and watch together. The sync was tight—usually within 50ms. Good enough that you could laugh at jokes simultaneously.

The Twilio blog published a full writeup of the implementation.

articles.title: Teaching Git to 500 Students: Lessons from GitHub Campus Expert
articles.excerpt: As a GitHub Campus Expert, I taught version control to hundreds of students. Here's what I learned about pedagogy, workshop design, and why git-intro-slides and workshop-guestbook were essential tools.
articles.content:
The Git Learning Curve

Git is simple until it's not. Students grasp add, commit, push quickly. Then they hit their first merge conflict and the illusion shatters. The DAG model, remote tracking branches, rebasing—these concepts don't fit in a 2-hour workshop.

You can't teach Git in an afternoon. You can teach enough Git to be dangerous.

The Workshop Format

I developed a specific rhythm for Git workshops:

  1. Concept (5 min): Why version control matters
  2. Demo (10 min): Instructor live-codes the basics
  3. Exercise (20 min): Students work in pairs
  4. Share (10 min): Groups present solutions
  5. Repeat: Next concept

The key: never more than 15 minutes of talking. Students learn Git by doing Git.

git-intro-slides

I built git-intro-slides—a reveal.js presentation with embedded terminal recordings. Students could follow along at their own pace, rewind the demos, and focus on concepts instead of typing speed.

workshop-guestbook

The biggest challenge: students need a safe repository to break. I created workshop-guestbook—a repo where every student adds their name via pull request. It teaches:

  • Forking
  • Branching
  • Pull requests
  • Merge conflicts (inevitable and educational)

By the end, students have made a real contribution to a real project. The dopamine hit of seeing "Merged" on their PR is worth more than any slide.

Lessons Learned

After 500+ students across a dozen workshops:

  • Start with GUI: GitKraken or GitHub Desktop reduces initial friction
  • Embrace failure: Conflicts are teaching moments, not emergencies
  • Context matters: CS students want theory; bootcamp students want jobs
  • Follow-up: One workshop isn't enough; create a community for continued learning

articles.title: slacker: A CMS Inside Slack
articles.excerpt: Hackathon project: why build a CMS in Slack? Because that's where teams already communicate. slacker turned Slack channels into a fully functional content management system.
articles.content:
The Insight

Content management systems are lonely places. You write in the CMS, then copy-paste to Slack to discuss. Or worse: discuss in Slack, then someone has to document decisions in the CMS.

What if the CMS was Slack?

The Build

In 24 hours, we built slacker:

  • Slack slash commands for CRUD operations
  • Message threads as content versions
  • Reactions as approval workflows
  • Bot DMs for structured content entry

How It Worked

/slacker create post "Q1 Update"
> Bot opens DM with content form
> You fill fields
> Bot posts to #content channel
> Team reacts with :white_check_mark: or :x:> Approved content auto-publishes to site

Why It Actually Made Sense

Traditional CMSs optimize for content creators. slacker optimized for teams. The context—discussions, decisions, approvals—lived alongside the content. No context switching. No lost threads.

We didn't win the hackathon, but the idea stuck. Today, tools like Notion and Coda blur the line between communication and content. We were just early.

articles.title: seefood: Hot Dog Detection with Machine Learning
articles.excerpt: The HBO Silicon Valley "Not Hotdog" app, made real. A subway food classifier that distinguishes hot dogs from other foods using computer vision.
articles.content:
The Joke

HBO's Silicon Valley featured an app that could tell if something was a hot dog. It was a punchline about startup culture—solving trivial problems with maximum engineering effort.

We decided the joke deserved to be real.

The Technical Challenge

Building a hot dog classifier sounds simple. It's a binary classification problem: hot dog or not hot dog. But the real world is messy:

  • Hot dogs in buns vs. out of buns
  • Corn dogs (technically hot dogs?)
  • Bratwurst, sausages, similar tube-shaped foods
  • Lighting, angles, backgrounds

The Model

We fine-tuned MobileNet on ~5,000 labeled images. The twist: we specifically included "confusing negatives"—foods that look like hot dogs but aren't. The model learned subtle features: bun texture, condiment patterns, casing sheen.

The Result

92% accuracy on the test set. 100% accuracy on the only metric that mattered: making people laugh when it correctly identified their lunch.

articles.title: Serverless Go: Contact Forms Without PHP
articles.excerpt: The serverless-go-forms project: handle contact form submissions with AWS Lambda and Go. No servers to manage, no PHP security nightmares.
articles.content:
The PHP Problem

Contact forms on static sites usually mean PHP. And PHP on shared hosting is a security disaster waiting to happen. Outdated versions, vulnerable mail() implementations, form spam.

I wanted contact forms without servers. Enter AWS Lambda.

The Architecture

serverless-go-forms provides a simple Lambda function that:

  1. Receives POST from static site via API Gateway
  2. Validates input (honeypot, rate limiting)
  3. Sends via SES or forwards to another endpoint
  4. Returns CORS-friendly response

Go on Lambda

Go compiles to a single binary. Cold starts are fast (~10ms). The runtime is minimal. Deployment is a ZIP upload.

func handler(ctx context.Context, req events.APIGatewayProxyRequest) (events.APIGatewayProxyResponse, error) {
    var form ContactForm
    if err := json.Unmarshal([]byte(req.Body), &form); err != nil {
        return response(400, "Invalid JSON"), nil
    }
    
    if err := validate(form); err != nil {
        return response(400, err.Error()), nil
    }
    
    if err := sendEmail(form); err != nil {
        return response(500, "Failed to send"), nil
    }
    
    return response(200, "Message sent"), nil
}

Cost

The Lambda free tier covers 1M requests/month. For a personal site, that's effectively free. Compare to $5-10/month for shared hosting.

articles.title: From Docker to Podman: A Migration Story
articles.excerpt: Migrating 200+ services from Docker to Podman. The why, the how, and lessons learned about daemonless containers and rootless security.
articles.content:
The Docker Dilemma

Docker changed how we deploy software. But the Docker daemon runs as root. A container escape means root on the host. In a multi-tenant environment, that's unacceptable.

We needed rootless containers. We needed Podman.

Why Podman?

Podman is daemonless. It runs containers as the invoking user. No root-owned daemon process. No socket to protect. Container escapes are contained to the user's privileges.

Additional wins:

  • systemd integration (podman generate systemd)
  • Kubernetes YAML compatibility (podman play kube)
  • Docker CLI compatibility (alias docker=podman)

The Migration Strategy

200+ services across development, staging, and production. We couldn't migrate everything at once.

  1. Dev first: Developers switched voluntarily
  2. CI/CD migration: GitLab runners updated to use podman
  3. Staging validation: Full test suite against podman
  4. Production rollout: Service-by-service with rollback plans

The Surprises

Not everything was smooth:

  • DNS resolution: Podman's default network differs from Docker's
  • Volume permissions: Rootless means UID mapping headaches
  • Health checks: Some Docker HEALTHCHECK patterns didn't translate

The Results

After 6 months, all 200+ services ran on Podman. Zero container escapes during the migration period. Developers adapted quickly—most didn't notice the difference after the first week.

The security team was happy. The infrastructure team was happy. The only unhappy people were the ones who had to write the migration documentation.

articles.title: nestjs-redis-streams
articles.excerpt: An open-source npm package providing Redis Streams integration for NestJS applications.
articles.content:
nestjs-redis-streams is an open-source npm package that provides easy integration of Redis Streams into NestJS applications. It offers decorators, message handlers, and configuration utilities for building event-driven microservices with Redis as the message broker.
